OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "I've been following this topic closely, especially since I'm an immigrant living in Bari with three children and a husband. The first thing that comes to mind is how much stress it can cause when you're constantly exposed to misinformation or overly curated lives online.\n\nAs someone married with kids who has only upper secondary education but my daughter has finished high school, I've learned not to take everything at face value. Life isn't like the pretty pictures we see on social media, and sometimes those fake posts can make people feel worse about their own situations. For instance, when I scroll through Instagram or Facebook, I find myself questioning how much of what's shared is real.\n\nBut dealing with it requires effort. We need to fact-check more often than just a quick glance at the source page because some content creators go out of their way to mislead. Also, being aware that algorithms might prioritize engagement over accuracy helps me understand why certain posts appear multiple times in my feed.",
  "reasoning_summary": "First, I considered how personal experiences align with dealing fake content—being an immigrant and having a family adds context.\n\nThen, I thought about the role of education: while formal schooling isn't extensive, practical life skills help navigate social media complexities.\n\nI also reflected on my high agreeableness but moderate to high neuroticism traits; being kind can make it hard to call out false content directly.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I've been following this topic closely, especially since I'm an immigrant living in Bari with three children and a husband. The first thing that comes to mind is how much stress it can cause when you're constantly exposed to misinformation or overly curated lives online.

As someone married with kids who has only upper secondary education but my daughter has finished high school, I've learned not to take everything at face value. Life isn't like the pretty pictures we see on social media, and sometimes those fake posts can make people feel worse about their own situations. For instance, when I scroll through Instagram or Facebook, I find myself questioning how much of what's shared is real.

But dealing with it requires effort. We need to fact-check more often than just a quick glance at the source page because some content creators go out of their way to mislead. Also, being aware that algorithms might prioritize engagement over accuracy helps me understand why certain posts appear multiple times in my feed.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
First, I considered how personal experiences align with dealing fake content—being an immigrant and having a family adds context.

Then, I thought about the role of education: while formal schooling isn't extensive, practical life skills help navigate social media complexities.

I also reflected on my high agreeableness but moderate to high neuroticism traits; being kind can make it hard to call out false content directly.
